segments.text: For being here.
We'll let you all get settled
and then we'll get set up into, uh,
public, public session.
Well, welcome everyone.
We are here, uh, to hear from the minister on the education
and training system, reform Amendment Bill.
Um, we've had all of our public hearings already. Mm-hmm.
Um, and so we are now to hear from you minister, um,
brief us on, on the bill.
Um, and the half an hour we have is your time.
Uh, does include time for questions over to you, uh,
to use that time as you wish.
And, um, we'll pass over to you now. Thank
You. Or can I just start by
saying welcome back?
Thank you. It's good to have you back.
Um, and Carl did a, a wonderful job, um, very much.
And I guess while we're all here, is for the reason
that you were away Yeah.
To, uh, improve our education system
and make sure all our kids live up to their,
their fullest potential, um, through the education system.
Um, so thank you very much for your hard work on this.
It's really actually quite interesting doing this the other
way round rather than coming in
before submissions being able to come in after.
Um, so that I have a really good idea.
So maybe it's something to think about for the future.
'cause I actually think this has been, uh,
probably slightly better in my view.
Thanks feedback, but save that up to, um, you, uh,
there are a number of changes in this bill,
but overall, if we helicopter up, it is about, uh, ensuring,
as I said earlier, that we are raising achievement
for our children.
That we are adding more accountability into the system.
Uh, and some of the changes are just good health
of a regulatory, uh, system as well.
So there's a number of changes.
Um, I think I'll start with probably the two changes
that you probably had most of the feedback on.
Um, I'll start with the teaching council,
because that's probably the one you had,
you did have quite a bit of feedback on.
I just start with a little bit of background on this.
One of my priority areas, of course, is to, uh,
develop the workforce of the future.
And one of the biggest concerns from the sector.
Uh, and the first thing that principals
and teachers usually speak to me about is the quality
of initial teacher education.
Uh, and that their feeling is that their experiences, that
that is not where it needs to be.
And that over time, the quality of the graduates coming
through is, uh, uh, you know, uh, decreasing over time.
Now that, uh, feeling is replicated in the evidence
that we've received and the reports that have been done, uh,
firstly by the Education Review Office, uh, where 60%
of principals report their new teachers are under prepared,
uh, primary school teachers report not being prepared
to teach all subjects.
Uh, and that 50% of new teachers, only 50%, found
that the ITE was effective, where almost 30% described it
as ineffective, and they felt unprepared for teaching.
So there's that.
There's the Rural Society Math Report that said that 45%
of teachers felt, uh,
that they were only moderately confident at teaching any
strand of mathematics in year four.
Uh, and then we've also got the findings from the TELUS
report as background, um,
an overall decrease in the proportion of recent graduates
who felt prepared since the last TELUS report.
Under half of teachers with less than five years
of experience reported feeling prepared for most aspects
of teaching well below the OECD average.
And so with that, the change that we have made on the back
of the Rural Society Math Report was requiring, of course,
level two mathematics for,
or equivalent for ITE,
which we're working through at the moment.
And it's been, uh, implemented.
So that is the, the, the backdrop.
The reality is that the government holds no levers
to make any changes to ITE.
We, I cannot, as the minister currently,
I don't hold any powers
or anything to be able to improve the, uh, the, uh,
initial teacher education programs, uh, as they exist.
So the, uh, proposals, uh, in this, keeping
that in mind, the, the key proposal, uh, is to ensure
that we do have a letter to grip this up as teachers
that ask us to do and have higher expectations
on initial teacher education.
Uh, and that is to set the standards required
for initial teacher education, move
that from the teaching council currently into the Ministry
of Education, uh,
and, uh, doing what they would normally do, which is
to consult with the sector, uh, of course, uh,
but to set much higher standards.
Now, when I have met with principals,
and if they're watching and teachers, I said to them,
when this moment arrives and this bill is
before the house, remember the
conversation I'm having with you now.
I don't hold any levers to improve ITE.
You are asking me to help you improve initial teacher
education to set teachers up for success.
This is what we're going to do, grip it up
and have much, uh, stronger settings to ensure that, uh,
ITA, uh, is improved.
The second part of the, of this is also a balancing act, uh,
to strengthen the, uh, legislative require powers
of the teaching council, on the other hand, to monitor, uh,
the, uh, ITT sector against the standards that we set.
So it is really good regulatory health of the system to have
standard setting and monitoring functions
and approval functions sitting in d in different,
sitting in different places.
Uh, and you see us doing
that throughout the education system,
trying to separate those out.
That healthy tension is very important.
Uh, so on the one hand,
removing the standard setting function on the others,
on the other hand, allowing the teaching council to have,
uh, more powers to require information from ITE providers,
uh, to explicitly set conditions on approvals, uh,
and ability to, to revoke approvals as well,
which is powers they have not had before.
So it's a, a balancing.
Um, another key aspect, of course, is the, uh,
that we are removing the standard setting function, uh,
of the, uh, teacher, uh, registration guidelines
and setting that inside the ministry as well.
Of course, same goes for when they're set to be, uh,
requiring them to work with the sector
and consultation to create those.
But they are another really key, important lever
to improve initial teacher education.
If those are strengthened, it's going to mean
that ITE providers have to lift their game in order
to get their students registered against really robust
standards, including things like, uh, you know,
understanding the curriculum material.
Because all of the things we are seeing is
that teachers are not prepared to teach maths.
They're not prepared to teach English.
They're not prepared to teach technology.
So we can see that there is a disconnect between
a good understanding of the curriculum, uh,
and being the requirement to become registered as a teacher.
So if we are, we grip that up as well, uh,
and make sure we're setting high standards,
the IT providers are going to have to meet those standards,
otherwise their graduates are not gonna be able
to get registered as teachers.
These are two core pieces of, uh,
or two core levers for us
to improve initial teacher education, which is
what the sector have been calling for.
Um, those are, uh, regulatory parts.
The second part of the changes to the teaching council,
and I, you will have seen the story in the paper today,
and I provided, um, you with a copy
of the Debbie Francis report.
I've not been able to talk about this obviously
before now, um, interesting timing.
But it is a extraordinarily
serious report, uh, that has
very strong implications.
Essentially, I sat down with the PSC at the end
of last year, the Public Service Commission, and
before I received the report.
And they, their words to me were
that the teaching Council did not view themself as a
child protection agency or a regulatory body.
And it was, uh, DP Francis' findings,
which are very concerning.
That they're more concerned with raising the status
of the profession and being friends
with the profession than their regulatory duty
of protecting children
and ensuring that teachers are meeting certain standards.
Those are very, very serious allegations and findings.
And, uh, I know that there will be, uh,
there will be more to come from this.
I am taking further advice from the ministry on,
on future policy settings now that this report is out,
because obviously these changes I propose in this bill were
made before, well before we had this report.
I don't think anyone in the sector will be surprised
at this report because the feedback
that I've had from principals across the country
and the anecdotal stories they tell me, uh, will mean
that they, they're not surprised by this.
And actually, if you want to raise the status
of the profession, you need to make sure
that teachers are meeting a standard
and that it is being done in a robust way.
So part of this bill, uh, is about, uh,
making sure that the teaching council are sticking
to their core duty, which is what the sector expect
of registering teachers and holding them to that standard.
And, uh, and also being a child protection agency.
So we're removing some of the peripheral stuff that they do,
uh, raising the status of the profession, some
of their leadership functions, their best practice evidence,
research pieces, which actually sit in lots
of other different places so that they can
focus on what they need to do.
And currently, what they are not doing,
which I'm very, very concerned about.
There are a number of other reports that are due out that,
um, the PSC and the Teaching Council are undertaking.
Mm-hmm. Um, and those will be out in due course as well.
But they all together will tell, uh, the story of why,
uh, we need to make sure,
or child protection reasons, we need to grip this up.
So those are the changes to the teaching council.
Uh, I just move into the other part, uh, which will,
I think is the, probably the other area that's the most, uh,
interest to the
submitters, and that's around the curriculum.
Uh, obviously one other, one
of my key areas is curriculum reform,
to make sure we have a world leading year
by year knowledge rich internationally best practice
curriculum that is sequenced, carefully sequenced.
Um, our message, the sector has always been,
this is here to stay.
What we've seen in the past is, uh,
through curriculum statements,
which have always been a thing,
curriculum statements have been around since the nineties,
since tomorrow's schools where, uh, the, uh,
minister has the ability to issue a curriculum statement.
Uh, what we've found is
that every 20 years, well,
so far there's been this homeless bolus change,
which is disruptive to the sector.
I acknowledge that where every curriculum area
is completely revamped.
'cause we haven't done anything in 20 years.
The world's shifted, topics have shifted,
information's shifted.
And, uh, we do this whole bolus change
of every area, and it is disruptive.
International best practice shows that a regular cadence of,
uh, small changes over time.
So you might see English, for example,
every five years sits on a regular cadence
of review sector feedback.
How are things going? What does
international best practice say?
What do the experts say?
What are the things we can tweak and change?
Uh, so you get this ongoing cadence
of small changes rather than a, you know, uh, uh,
a wholesale change every 20 years, which is disruptive.
And so the message is very clearly
that these changes are here to stay.
We do not wanna be disruptive.
Um, and, uh, we are getting in line
with international best practice in terms of those changes.
Uh, so there's no new power, uh,
around issuing a curriculum statement.
That's just the way it's always been.
But we are also introducing, uh, which is currently
what we do, 'cause it's best practice,
but it's not in, uh, legislation to make sure

and a range of other things, which is
currently not in the legislation.
So, um, that's so relevant.
Evidence from new developments
and educational research, international best practice
information about the educational achievements.
All these things need to be taken into account,
which is currently what we are doing, uh, is
because it is best practice,
but we think it should be, uh, from the legislation.
Yep. So those are the two main things.

moving the functions from the, uh,
property department into a, um, uh, an entity,
a crown entity, because we want to continue
and improve, uh, the value for money

and Lee, uh, to drive that down.

we have done an extraordinarily good job of,
of getting good value for money.

that was delivered for $41 million for 700 students.

we'd already spent with the, you know,
previous governments had spent $5 million on a plan

Uh, we delivered state of the art beautiful classroom,
three story block, absolutely beautiful
for 41 million for 700 students.
That's what we can do, uh, when we, uh, really, uh,
you know, have a, our eye on the ball.
And we will do that more so with a, uh, an agency.

and also making sure that schools are required to do PS

Secondly, in terms of setting those, uh, standards

Because if we're going to set high standards for it

Uh, and there will be a standard setting

This is one of the only things that we can do, uh,

That's how we got to this position.

More than 90%

I can't go into detail around some of the other things
'cause they're under active consideration.
But again, I can just promise you they are not the things,
the key levers.
If there was something that I could do,
and I think the previous government to be fair,
probably heard the same things,
minister Ens probably heard the same thing.
If there was something we could do that ensured
that the ITE providers lifted their game,
we would have done it.
And we have relied on the teaching council
and their standard settings and they have not gripped it up.
I have provided, uh, my thoughts, uh, to them
on where I think they need to go to grip this up
and to raise standards and expectations on ITE, uh,
and, uh, that, uh, has not happened.
So if we want to do right by teachers
and provide them with the ITE experience,
that sets them up for success.
We have to set higher standards.
But I would like to also say, remembering
that I am also giving the council enhanced powers
that they have asked me for.
Because at the moment, it's kind of a handshake they have
with the IT providers to be able to do anything.
I'm now giving them legislative powers to go,
these are the standards that have been set.
I'm requiring you to give me the information.
You say you're doing structured literacy,
but actually it turns out, oh, you're only actually doing it
for one hour over a year course.
Is that really teaching structured literacy?
So they'll have more powers to go in
to properly look at exactly what's happening in the course,
um, that they have been asking for.
So it is a balance of giving them more power,
but it is regulatory best practice not
to have standard setting and monitoring
and enforcement sitting in the same body.
Uh, and we're doing that with, in, with, uh, the arrow
and ECE functions as well.
Standard setting, sitting with the ministry enforcement
and monitoring and licensing sitting with them.
So it is that healthy tension in the system.
It is best practice
and I understand the concerns of those submitters.
But if we want to raise, uh,
the expectations on initial teacher education,
properly set up our teachers for success,
then we've gotta grip this up
with respect to both sides of the table.
Carl, if you can make it succinct,
I'll give the last question.
Thanks, chair. Uh, minister.
So we've heard a bit about the concern around moving, uh,
property into the New Crown agency.
Um, you've shared an example of the significant saving
by planning these things properly.
Can you just explain for the committee
what the impact on students is gonna be
of there being more money
to spend on property and maintenance? Yeah,
But we are massively behind and have been for decades
and decades in maintenance and role growth.
We've just put together the very first 10 year property plan
in terms of maintenance and growth we've ever had.
So we can clearly see what are the needs over time
and if different budget decisions are made,
what are the key trade offs?
Um, 'cause that is really good transparency in the system.
Um, it, it basically, there will be, uh,
a lot more professionalism of, uh, the entity, a board
of experts who have had between them decades and decades
and decades of experience.
Uh, so you will see us being able
to provide more classrooms, more upgrades, more maintenance,
uh, but also a bit more flexibility.
There are a number of schools that right now,
frankly build classrooms themselves
faster and cheaper than we do.
Uh, so there will be an opportunity for that new fun,
that new entity to be able to take more calculated risks.
But also in terms of small, rural, isolated schools
or other schools that just don't want to deal with property,
how do we bucket those up
and say to them, look, there is a function
that this entity will take on and take that off your hands.
There's a lot of things that they will be able to do, uh,
to make it easier for schools, uh, give more flexibility,
but in the end, drive better value
for money than we're even seeing at the moment.
More efficiency, far better communication, um, with schools,
because that has been a big problem.
What we're trying to get away from is
what we found when we became the government.
There was all these announcements that had been made
and promises and schools were expecting things.
There was no funding, zero funding.
The school that we opened the other day,
there was no funding for.
Um, we announced that funding and budget. It's
True. You could have easily put,
We, we budgeted for in 2024.
As I say, we delivered it 41 million rather, 76. It's not.
And I expect actually that over time we'll be able
to drive that down even more.
So we're getting better value for money.
We're building more classrooms,
we're maintaining more classrooms, uh,
and that that entity will, will take that even further.
Thank you very much, minister,
for making yourself available to us.
Thank you to see you. Uh, I've just got e to look into it.
So she's just following up. Mr.
Not that e can see. I haven't checked my emails though while
I've been doing this, so we'll check after this.
Thank you.
